title: REST API
content:
Zaroor! Hum is playlist ke flow ke saath hi aage badhenge. Kyunki mere paas Video #2 (pycache) aur Video #7 (Database Setup) ke sources available nahi hain, hum un topics ko skip karke baaki saare available sources ko detail mein cover karenge.

Chaliye shuru karte hain Introduction aur Video #1 se.

1. Introduction: REST API Development with Python Flask

Sabse pehle samajhte hain ki hum kya banane ja rahe hain. Flask ek Python library hai jo humein REST APIs banane ke liye zaroori tools aur ek aasaan syntax provide karti hai.

Key Concepts:

  • Endpoints: Jaise youtube.com/playlist, yahan /playlist ek endpoint hai.
  • HTTP Methods: Hum seekhenge ki kaise GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, aur PATCH methods ka use karke data ke saath interact kiya jata hai.
  • Scalability: Sirf code likhna kaafi nahi hai; hum apne code ko Scalable aur Maintainable banayenge (MVC Architecture use karke) taaki jab project bada ho, toh use manage karna aasaan ho.

2. Video #1: Creating Virtual Environment & First Flask App

Koi bhi professional project shuru karne se pehle ek Virtual Environment banana bahut zaroori hai.

Step 1: Virtual Environment Kyun?

Virtual environment isliye banate hain taaki hamare project ki libraries (jaise Flask) hamare Operating System ke Global Environment se alag rahein. Isse version conflicts nahi hote aur hamara OS effect nahi hota.

Step 2: Setup Commands (Windows)

  1. Apne project folder mein terminal open karein.
  2. Environment Create karein: 
    python -m venv venv
    (Yahan venv environment ka naam hai).
  3. Environment Activate karein: 
    .\venv\Scripts\activate
    Activation ke baad aapko terminal prompt mein (venv) dikhega.
  4. lib ke andar dikhenge file 
agar humdekhenge ki venv ke andar scripts hota h uske andar activate hota h iske liye yeh aisa run krne ka bna

Step 3: Flask Install Karna

Environment activate karne ke baad Flask install karein:

pip install flask

Isse Flask aur uski zaroori dependencies aapke environment mein download ho jayengi.

by DEFAULT FLASK APP.PY DHUNDTA H 

Step 4: Pehla Flask App (app.py)

Ab ek file banaiye app.py aur ye code likhiye:

from flask import Flask # Flask class ko import kiya (class h)

app = Flask(__name__) # App ka instance banaya

// sirf inte se code chl jaayega service start hojayegi /
/ humra phla endpoint hoga # Pehla Endpoint (Root)
@app.route("/") # Ye ek decorator hai jo URL path define karta hai
# iske niche wala code / pe hit karne pe run hoga 
def hello_world():
    return "Hello World" # Browser mein ye message dikhega

# Dusra Endpoint (Home)
@app.route("/home")
def home_page():
    return "This is Home Page"

Step 5: App ko Run karna

App run karne ke liye terminal mein type karein:

flask run

By default, ye http://127.0.0.1:5000/ par chalta hai.

Node.js

Set:

export NODE_ENV=development

Step 6: Development Mode Setup

Agar aap code mein change karte hain, toh bar-bar server stop karke start karna padta hai. Isse bachne ke liye hum Development Environment set karte hain taaki server automatic restart ho jaye:

Command:

set FLASK_ENV=development

Iske baad flask run karne par server Debug Mode mein chalega aur har save par auto-reload hoga.


$env:FLASK_ENV="development" for cmd

Extra Note: Jab aap app.route("/") use karte hain, toh ise Base URL kehte hain. Iske baad aane wale words (jaise /home) ko Endpoints kaha jata hai.

Agar aapko ye samajh aa gaya hai aur aap agle topic (Video #3: Creating Multiple Files) par jaana chahte hain, toh please proceed kahein.


-__________________________________________________________________________________

NOTE

hum jb bhi pip install krteh toh venv se bhar karte h toh globlly hota h 

under venv krte h toh local me install hota h 

----------------

and agar node js ki baat kar rhe h toh node js me toh node modules hote h m jb 

npm install package name krunga toh sirf usi file ke lyie hoga agar global chaiye h toh i do like this

npm install -g package_name


pip = pip install package

set PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1

_________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Chaliye, ab hum Video #3 ki taraf badhte hain jo aapko ek basic coder se ek Professional Backend Developer banane ki pehli seedhi hai.

Video #3: Creating Multiple Files in Flask

Abhi tak humne saara code app.py mein likha tha. Lekin real-world projects mein, ek hi file mein hazaron lines ka code likhna impossible hai. Is video mein hum seekhenge ki code ko Organize aur Split kaise karte hain.

jsese database me alag alag table h toh uske liye hum alag alag file bnarhe honge 

1. Intuition: Humein Multiple Files Kyun Chahiye?

  • Scalability: Agar aap ek E-commerce app bana rahe hain, toh usmein Users, Products, aur Payments jaise alag-alag modules honge. Ek hi file mein sab likhne se code "Messy" ho jayega.
  • Collaboration: Agar 3 developers hain, toh ek User module pe kaam kar sakta hai, dusra Products pe. Alag files hone se wo ek dusre ke code ko disturb nahi karenge.
  • Maintainability: Kal ko agar sirf 'User Login' mein bug aata hai, toh aapko pata hoga ki sirf user_controller.py file check karni hai.

2. Code Implementation: Moving to Controller

hum mvc structure follow kar rhe h model view controler
model me actuall buisness logi hoga jise hum aage jaake database se interact karenge 
controller me hum vesa code likhenge jo routes handle karega

Hum ek nayi file banayenge user_controller.py aur app.py se logic wahan shift karenge.

Step 1: user_controller.py banana Is file mein hum User se related endpoints rakhenge.


abhi humara app.py entry point user_controller.py kese chelga fir 

user_controller.py ko app.py me import karunga  

uske liye phle app.py me import krna hoga 


baadme krna pdega joonsiline me app insitialse hua h uske baad hi likh rha hounga m 

but itne se kaam nhi krega 


yeh khra h ki humne toh is app ko janta hi nahi 

ab app reintailse karenge toh voh flask pagla jaayega

from app import app

phla app --> app.py ; import app (app object ka naam )


# user_controller.py mein hum app object ko import karenge
from app import app

@app.route("/user/signup")
def user_signup():
    return "This is User Signup Operation"

Step 2: app.py ko Update karna app.py hamara entry point rahega. Humein yahan batana padega ki humne dusri files mein bhi routes likhe hain.

from flask import Flask
app = Flask(__name__)

# Apne controller ko yahan import karna zaroori hai
# taaki Flask ko pata chale ki naye routes kahan hain
import user_controller

@app.route("/")
def index():
    return "Main App Page"

phle use import karenge us file ko humeri app.py me ki woh chle uske baad hum us file me jaake from app import app karenge


3. The "Circular Import" Challenge (Very Important)

Jab aap app.py mein import user_controller karte hain aur user_controller.py mein from app import app karte hain, toh ise Circular Import kehte hain.

Solution: Hamesha import user_controller ko app = Flask(__name__) ke baad likhein. Isse Flask pehle app instance bana leta hai, aur phir controller ko wo instance mil jata hai.

💡 Interview Ready: Hot Questions!

Backend interviews mein ye sawal aksar pooche jate hain:

  1. Question: Flask mein "Circular Import" error kya hota hai aur ise kaise handle karte hain?

    • Answer: Jab do files ek dusre ko import karne ki koshish karti hain, toh system confuse ho jata hai. Flask mein ise handle karne ke liye hum imports ko file ke end mein ya app instance create hone ke baad rakhte hain. Professional tareeke mein hum Blueprints (jo hum aage seekhenge) ka use karte hain.

  2. Question: Monolithic vs. Modular Structure mein kya farak hai?

    • Answer: Monolithic ka matlab hai saara code ek hi file/place par hona, jo manage karna mushkil hai. Modular structure (jaise hum abhi kar rahe hain) mein code ko logic ke hisaab se alag files mein divide kiya jata hai, jo scalability ke liye best hai.

  3. Question: app.py ko hum hamesha Entry Point kyun kehte hain?

    • Answer: Kyunki Flask server isi file se start hota hai aur yahi file baaki saare controllers aur models ko bind karti hai.

Kya aapne ye structure setup kar liya hai? Agar haan, toh hum agle part (Video #4 & #5: Packages & Automation) par chal sakte hain jahan hum ise aur bhi professional banayenge. Proceed?


Chaliye, ab hum Video #3 ki taraf badhte hain jo aapko ek basic coder se ek Professional Backend Developer banane ki pehli seedhi hai.

Video #3: Creating Multiple Files in Flask

Abhi tak humne saara code app.py mein likha tha. Lekin real-world projects mein, ek hi file mein hazaron lines ka code likhna impossible hai. Is video mein hum seekhenge ki code ko Organize aur Split kaise karte hain.

1. Intuition: Humein Multiple Files Kyun Chahiye?

  • Scalability: Agar aap ek E-commerce app bana rahe hain, toh usmein Users, Products, aur Payments jaise alag-alag modules honge. Ek hi file mein sab likhne se code "Messy" ho jayega.
  • Collaboration: Agar 3 developers hain, toh ek User module pe kaam kar sakta hai, dusra Products pe. Alag files hone se wo ek dusre ke code ko disturb nahi karenge.
  • Maintainability: Kal ko agar sirf 'User Login' mein bug aata hai, toh aapko pata hoga ki sirf user_controller.py file check karni hai.

2. Code Implementation: Moving to Controller

Hum ek nayi file banayenge user_controller.py aur app.py se logic wahan shift karenge.

Step 1: user_controller.py banana Is file mein hum User se related endpoints rakhenge.

# user_controller.py mein hum app object ko import karenge
from app import app

@app.route("/user/signup")
def user_signup():
    return "This is User Signup Operation"

Step 2: app.py ko Update karna app.py hamara entry point rahega. Humein yahan batana padega ki humne dusri files mein bhi routes likhe hain.

from flask import Flask
app = Flask(__name__)

# Apne controller ko yahan import karna zaroori hai
# taaki Flask ko pata chale ki naye routes kahan hain
import user_controller

@app.route("/")
def index():
    return "Main App Page"

3. The "Circular Import" Challenge (Very Important)

Jab aap app.py mein import user_controller karte hain aur user_controller.py mein from app import app karte hain, toh ise Circular Import kehte hain.

Solution: Hamesha import user_controller ko app = Flask(__name__) ke baad likhein. Isse Flask pehle app instance bana leta hai, aur phir controller ko wo instance mil jata hai.

💡 Interview Ready: Hot Questions!

Backend interviews mein ye sawal aksar pooche jate hain:

  1. Question: Flask mein "Circular Import" error kya hota hai aur ise kaise handle karte hain?

    • Answer: Jab do files ek dusre ko import karne ki koshish karti hain, toh system confuse ho jata hai. Flask mein ise handle karne ke liye hum imports ko file ke end mein ya app instance create hone ke baad rakhte hain. Professional tareeke mein hum Blueprints (jo hum aage seekhenge) ka use karte hain.

  2. Question: Monolithic vs. Modular Structure mein kya farak hai?

    • Answer: Monolithic ka matlab hai saara code ek hi file/place par hona, jo manage karna mushkil hai. Modular structure (jaise hum abhi kar rahe hain) mein code ko logic ke hisaab se alag files mein divide kiya jata hai, jo scalability ke liye best hai.

  3. Question: app.py ko hum hamesha Entry Point kyun kehte hain?

    • Answer: Kyunki Flask server isi file se start hota hai aur yahi file baaki saare controllers aur models ko bind karti hai.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Video #4: Creating Packages in Flask to Segregate Files
Backend development mein jab projects bade hote hain, toh sirf files alag karna kaafi nahi hota. Humein unhe relevant folders mein "Group" karna padta hai. Isse hum MVC (Model-View-Controller) architecture ki taraf badhte hain. instead of monlith mtlb monolith mtlb ek hi file me saar code ;
1. Intuition: Packages Kyun Zaruri Hain?
Sochiye aapke paas 50 controllers aur 50 models hain. Agar sab ek hi root folder mein honge, toh file dhoondna nightmare ban jayega.
  • Segregation: Users se related files ek folder mein, Products ki dusre mein.
  • Professional Structure: Ye industry standard hai. Isse code scalable banta hai.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Step-by-Step Implementation
Step 1: Folder Structure Banana Apne project root mein ek naya folder banaiye: controller.
Step 2: Files Move Karna Apni purani user_controller.py file ko cut karke is controller folder ke andar paste kar dein.
Step 3: app.py mein Import Update Karna Kyunki ab file ka raasta (path) badal gaya hai, humein import statement change karni hogi.
# app.py
from flask import Flask
app = Flask(__name__)

# Pehle ye 'import user_controller' tha
# Ab folder name ke saath aayega
from controller import user_controller
Step 4: Naya Controller Add Karna (Testing ke liye) Ab hum ek aur file banate hain controller/product_controller.py taaki hum segregation dekh sakein.
# controller/product_controller.py
from app import app

@app.route("/product/add")
def product_add():
    return "Product Added Successfully"
Step 5: Dono Controllers ko app.py mein register karna
# app.py
from flask import Flask
app = Flask(__name__)

from controller import user_controller
from controller import product_controller # Naya import
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
dekho hum pura naam derhe h 

from app import app ko change nh i krna kyuki woh global chij hi h 
3. Video Flow ka Twist: __init__.py ka Janam
Jab aap ek folder banate hain aur usse code import karte hain, toh Python ke purane versions mein wahan ek khaas file ki zarurat hoti hai: __init__.py. Halanki naye Python versions mein ye optional hai, par professional projects mein ise hamesha rakha jata hai taaki folder ko ek Package ki tarah treat kiya ja sake.

  • __init__.py ek package initialization file hai.
  • Purane Python mein mandatory thi.
  • Modern Python mein optional hai.
  • Professional projects mein clarity aur package setup ke liye aksar rakhi jati hai.
  • Package import hote hi uska code execute hota hai.
  • Flask, Django aur bade Python projects mein tum ise bahut frequently dekhoge.

    • itna bhi nhi hota nexessary kyuki jb hum 
    from contrroler import * karenge toh wha pe hume pacakage ki jrurat pdhegi define karne ke liye 

    Modern Python (3.3+) mein __init__.py mandatory nahi hai, isliye agar tumhara structure:

    controller/├── product_controller.py├── user_controller.py

    hai, to ye kaam kar sakta hai:

    from controller import product_controller, user_controller

    kyunki Python controller ko namespace package ki tarah treat kar leta hai.

    Lekin jab tum likhte ho:

    from controller import *

    to Python ko ye jaana hota hai ki controller package se star import mein kya-kya export karna hai.

    Ye kaam normally controller/__init__.py handle karti hai:

    from .product_controller import *from .user_controller import *

    Ya:

    __all__ = ["product_controller", "user_controller"]

    Isliye:

    • ✅ from controller import product_controller
    • ✅ import controller.product_controller
    • ❓ from controller import * (behavior __init__.py par depend kar sakta hai aur generally recommended nahi hai)

    Ek aur practical point:


    --------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    💡 Interview Ready: Expert Questions!
    1. Question: Python mein ek folder aur ek 'Package' mein kya farak hai?
      • Answer: Ek normal folder sirf files store karta hai. Lekin agar folder ke andar __init__.py file hai, toh Python use ek "Package" ki tarah dekhta hai, jisse hum uske andar ki modules ko . (dot) notation se import kar sakte hain (e.g., from controller import user_controller).
    2. Question: Segregation se Flask app ki performance par kya asar padta hai?
      • Answer: Performance par direct asar nahi padta, lekin Developer Productivity aur Debugging Speed badh jati hai. Aapko pata hota hai ki error kaunse folder ki kaunsi file mein hai.
    3. Question: Agar main app.py mein 100 controllers manually import karun, toh kya ye galat hai?
      • Answer: Galat nahi hai, par ye Bad Practice hai. Itne saare manual imports app.py ko ganda kar dete hain. Iska solution hum agle video (Video #5) mein dekhenge jahan hum automation seekhenge.
    --------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    Extra Note: Hamesha yaad rakhein, backend developer ka kaam sirf code likhna nahi, balki code ko is tarah organize karna hai ki 6 mahine baad koi naya developer aaye toh use sab kuch crystal clear dikhe.
    Ab hum taiyaar hain Video #5 ke liye, jahan hum seekhenge ki kaise ek hi line mein saare controllers ko import kar lein.

    INIT.py

    The Solution: __init__.py and __all__
    Step 1: Create __init__.py Apne controller folder ke andar ek nayi file banaiye jiska naam ho __init__.py. Ye file Python ko batati hai ki ye folder ek Package hai.
    Step 2: Understanding __all__ Is file ke andar hum ek special list banate hain jiska naam hota hai __all__. Is list mein hum un saari files ke naam likhte hain jinhe hum bahar export karna chahte hain.
    # controller/__init__.py
__all__ = ["user_controller", "product_controller"]
    Step 3: The Automation Trick (Professional Approach) Manually list likhna bhi boring hai. Hum chahte hain ki Python khud folder scan kare aur saari .py files dhoond le. Iske liye hum __init__.py mein ye dynamic code likhte hain:
    # controller/__init__.py (Automated Code)
import os
import glob

# Current folder ki saari .py files ki list nikalna
# Aur unke naam se '.py' extension hata dena
files = glob.glob(os.path.dirname(__file__) + "/*.py")
__all__ = [os.path.basename(f)[:-3] for f in files if not f.endswith('__init__.py')]
    • Kaise kaam karta hai? Ye code folder mein har file ka path uthata hai, uska .py extension kaat deta hai, aur use __all__ list mein daal deta hai.
    Step 4: Update app.py Ab aapko app.py mein 10 imports likhne ki zaroorat nahi hai. Sirf ye ek line kaafi hai:
    # app.py
from controller import * # Isse saare controllers automatically import ho jayenge!
    1. Question: Python mein __init__.py file ka kya use hota hai?
      • Answer: __init__.py ek folder ko "Python Package" banati hai. Isse hum us folder se modules import kar sakte hain. Ye file package initialize hone par pehle run hoti hai.
    2. Question: from package import * kaise kaam karta hai?
      • Answer: Ye statement package ki __init__.py file mein jaakar __all__ list check karti hai. Us list mein jo bhi modules ke naam hote hain, unhe import kar liya jata hai.
    3. Question: Dynamic Import ka kya fayda hai?
      • Answer: Isse Scalability badhti hai. Developer ko baar-baar entry point (app.py) mein changes nahi karne padte. Nayi file add karte hi system use automatically recognize kar leta hai.
    4. Question: os aur glob modules yahan kya kar rahe hain?
      • Answer: os module paths ke saath khelne ke liye hai, aur glob module filesystem mein patterns (jaise *.py) ko dhoondne ke liye use hota hai.
    --------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    Extra Note: Hamesha yaad rakhein ki from controller import * use karte waqt __init__.py mein __all__ set hona mandatory hai, warna Python ko pata nahi chalega ki aap kya-kya import karna chahte hain.

    Ab hum aate hain Video #6 par, jo aapke project ki "Reer ki Haddi" (Backbone) hai—MVC Architecture.

    Video #6: Creating Controller Model Architecture in Flask

    Professional programming mein hum saara code ek hi jagah nahi bharte. Is video mein hum seekhenge ki code ko Controller aur Model mein kaise divide karte hain.

    1. Intuition: Controller aur Model ka Kaam Kya Hai?

    Backend development mein responsibilities divide hoti hain:

    • Controller: Ye sirf "Traffic Control" karta hai. Iska kaam hai request receive karna aur use sahi Model function ke paas bhej dena. Ye kabhi khud database se baat nahi karta.
    • Model: Ye "Core Engine" hai. Saara Business Logic (database queries, calculations, validation) Model ke andar hota hai.

    2. Implementation: Structuring the App

    Step 1: model Folder Banana Apne project root mein ek naya folder banaiye: model.

    Step 2: user_model.py banana Industry standard ke hisaab se Model ko ek Class ki tarah likha jata hai.

    # model/user_model.py

class user_model():
    def user_signup_model(self):
        # Yahan hum aage jaakar actual database query likhenge
        return "This is User Signup Model (Actual Business Logic)"


    ABHI AISE DIKH RHA H AAP DEKHSKTE HO KI AB HUME RETURN SE USER_MODEL.PY BOLKE CONNECT KRWANA H 

    toh hum controller ke return me modal ko call krenge 

    toh user_controller me m phle import karunga from model.user_model import user_model

    and us class ka object banlunga and by this objece me return me call krugna kyuki isme specific request jarhi hogi isse common nahi particular request 


    Step 3: user_controller.py ko Update karna Ab Controller request receive karega aur Model ko call karega.

    # controller/user_controller.py
from app import app
from model.user_model import user_model # Model class ko import kiya

# Model ka instance (object) banaya
obj = user_model()

@app.route("/user/signup")
def user_signup_controller():
    # Controller ne request pakdi aur Model ko request forward kar di
    return obj.user_signup_model()

    3. The Flow of Request (Pura Chakar)

    Video ke flow ke hisaab se request aise chalti hai:

    1. Browser: Request bhejta hai http://127.0.0.1:5000/user/signup.
    2. Controller: @app.route se us request ko pakadta hai.
    3. Model: Controller obj.user_signup_model() ko call karta hai.
    4. Database (Future): Model database se data laakar Controller ko deta hai.
    5. Response: Controller wahi data browser ko wapas bhej deta hai.

    💡 Interview Ready: High-Level Questions!

    1. Question: MVC architecture mein Controller aur Model ke beech kya difference hai?

      • Answer: Controller request receive karta hai aur response bhejta hai (receptionist), jabki Model actual data processing aur database operations karta hai (kitchen/engine).

    2. Question: Hamare architecture mein 'V' (View) kahan hai?

      • Answer: REST APIs mein hum sirf Data (JSON) bhejte hain, HTML pages nahi. Isliye 'View' frontend framework (jaise React ya Angular) handle karta hai. Hamara API sirf Model aur Controller par focus karta hai.

    3. Question: Scalable apps ke liye ye structure kyun zaroori hai?

      • Answer: Agar kal ko humein database badalna pade (SQL se NoSQL), toh humein sirf Model files change karni padengi, Controller (routes) waise hi rahenge. Isse code Maintainable banta hai.

    Extra Note: Hamesha yaad rakhein, ek "Great Coder" wahi hai jo minimal code Controller mein rakhe aur saara heavy logic Model mein shift kare. Ise hum "Skinny Controller, Fat Model" kehte hain.

    Kyunki hamare paas Video #7 (Database Setup) ka source nahi hai, hum seedha Video #8 par jayenge jahan hum seekhenge ki Python ko MySQL Database se kaise connect karte hain.

    Proceed?

    Video #7: Database Setup with MySQL Workbench & Creating Tables

    In this stage, we move from writing code to setting up the place where our data will live—the Database.
    1. Intuition: Why a Database?
    Until now, our API only returned static strings (e.g., "Hello World"). In a real app, you need to save user details (Signups, Logins).
    • MySQL: This is our Database Management System (DBMS).
    • MySQL Workbench: This is a Graphic User Interface (GUI) tool. Instead of writing complex commands to see your data, you can see it in tables like an Excel sheet.
    --------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    2. Step-by-Step Setup
    Step 1: Create a Schema (Database) A schema is like a "Project Folder" for your tables.
    • Open MySQL Workbench.
    • Click the "Create a new schema" icon.
    • Name it: flask_tutorial (This name is used in our connection code later).
    Step 2: Create the users Table Inside the schema, we need a table to store user information.
    • Columns & Data Types:
      1. id: Type INT. Set it as Primary Key (PK) and Auto Increment (AI). This ensures every user has a unique number.
      2. name: Type VARCHAR(100). Stores the user's name.
      3. email: Type VARCHAR(100). Stores the email address.
      4. phone: Type VARCHAR(20). Stores contact numbers.
      5. role: Type VARCHAR(50). Stores if they are an admin or user.
      6. password: Type VARCHAR(255). Stores the user's password.
    --------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    3. SQL Syntax (For your knowledge)
    While you can use the Workbench GUI, as a great backend developer, you should know the SQL command used behind the scenes:
    CREATE DATABASE flask_tutorial;
USE flask_tutorial;

CREATE TABLE users (
    id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100),
    email VARCHAR(100),
    phone VARCHAR(20),
    role VARCHAR(50),
    password VARCHAR(255)
);
    --------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    💡 Interview Ready: Database Essentials!
    1. Question: What is a Primary Key and why is Auto-Increment used?
      • Answer: A Primary Key is a unique identifier for a record in a table. It cannot be null and must be unique. Auto-Increment is used so the database automatically assigns a new number (1, 2, 3...) to every new record, saving the developer from manually managing IDs.
    2. Question: Difference between VARCHAR and TEXT?
      • Answer: VARCHAR is used for shorter strings (like names or emails) and is more performance-efficient as it has a set limit. TEXT is used for very long data (like blog posts) where the length is unpredictable.
    3. Question: Why do we separate the Database from the Application code?
      • Answer: This is called Decoupling. It allows the database to be hosted on a different server for better security and scalability. If the app crashes, the data remains safe in the database.
    --------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    Extra Note: In later videos (Video #21), we will modify this table to include Foreign Keys for more advanced authorization. But for now, this simple table is enough to start CRUD operations.
    Now that our database is ready, let's learn how to connect Python to this database in Video #8.
